title: My Rack (42U Homelab)
description:
My rack is a fully self-built 42U homelab that I built from scratch together with my father. The goal was to create an organized, maintainable infrastructure where networking, virtualization, monitoring, and daily services are managed in one controlled setup.

The system combines networking, virtualization, storage, monitoring, and security into a single homelab environment. An integrated UniFi dashboard runs on an embedded display, so I can follow the live network status and manage the system in real time. Security camera monitoring is also available through the same interface.

The rack setup includes:
- UniFi CloudKey Gen2 Plus for centralized UniFi controller management.
- A UDM Pro-Max router connected to the ONT fiber line.
- Proxmox (single-node) for virtualization.
- SFP+ switches (10 Gb/s) for fast and secure network segmentation.
- PoE switches for Wi-Fi access points, cameras, and sensors.
- Patch panels and structured cabling for a clean and serviceable layout.
- An UPS module to keep the most important systems running and to avoid downtime during power interruptions.

For self-hosted services, Proxmox runs virtual machines:
Pi-hole, VPN, Wazuh Security, Radarr/Sonarr, Home Assistant, and Nextcloud.

In addition, a Dell PowerEdge R530 is used for testing and lab experiments. It supports tasks like clustering, backups, and disaster recovery so I can safely try new configurations.

For sensors and connectivity, a mini-PC acts as a bridge for the sensor hub and also provides LTE failover WAN (4G).

All case fans are Noctua Chromax models to keep airflow strong while maintaining quiet operation. This homelab is designed to be reliable, modular, and easy to expand.

title: IT Lab Support and Infrastructure Setup
description:
After I completed my internship and finished my initial period at Dumobat, they contacted me again to help resolve several IT issues. The company already had most devices purchased and partially configured, but the infrastructure was not set up correctly in practice. Their switches, router, and other network equipment were all placed in a cabinet and needed a full reorganization and proper configuration.

At the same time, they had purchased a racing simulator that had to be configured from zero, including hardware setup, wiring, and system configuration before it could be used reliably.

They also had older devices affected by electrical overcharge events. Multiple power supplies were burned. I managed to recover and save one Synology NAS and two computers. Some monitors were also damaged by the overcharge; I investigated replacement options down to socket-level repair, but in that case it was not economically worth it.

Overall, my role was to stabilize and restructure their environment: clean up the rack and network layout, correct misconfigurations, recover as much hardware as possible, and bring critical systems back to a reliable working state.

title: Oogcentrum Baron Joostensstraat
description: This clinic started with no IT setup, so I handled the project from planning to delivery. I installed and configured two clinic PCs, built the website, and set up an internal app for patient flow and staff updates. I chose a compact Synology NAS to keep data on-site and make daily maintenance simple. On location, I assembled the hardware, installed Windows 11, and configured Synology DSM with shared storage and backups. The website supports online appointments, doctor selection, email confirmations, reminders, and cancellations. The admin area includes calendar scheduling, patient notes, user management, and real-time status updates during check-in and treatment.
